evidence_quotes.quote: The consumer may withdraw the licence for games held in his digital library and refuse access if the company so wishes, on the grounds of economic factors, or censorship

paragraphs.text: I am openly concerned about the situation of digital games, I will be concise in what I say: 19 years ago, Nintendo Wii came to the market, a console I still have to date, 2025, and has travelled with me to Spain. 19 years ago, my Wii came with a physical disc, containing Mario Kart Wii, a game that went next to the console. Today, 19 years later, and in April 2025, Nintendo Switch 2 was released and I will set an example by being from the same company. Switch 2, as I understand it, although the purchase with the pack containing Mario Kart World, NOT comes in physical. And this is not bad, the internet has become overcrowded, but the bad is what this entails, due to the fact that companies carry out their practices. Digital games are more convenient, and as they do not have to be imported, they are cheaper, but this does not mean you can play them in 20 years, as I could do today with my Wii, and my copy of Mario Kart Wii. Moreover, if you contact me, I’ll quote me, and live, connect my Wii and put some game for you to see, the game is mine, and I have not lost it. Digital games cease to exist when companies no longer feel profitable. This is the case of The Crew, Concord, and many other games that have been stuck, and have ceased to exist directly due to lack of preservation and anti-consumer policies. And what anti-consumer policies, specifically? Here we could talk about the price, but it is irrelevant in view of the fact that in both Epic Games and the Nintendo eShop de la Switch 1 and 2, PlayStation and Xbox, and Microsoft Store in Windows, there is nothing in the terms and conditions that you say: ‘The consumer may withdraw the licence for games held in his digital library and refuse access if the company so wishes, on the grounds of economic factors, or censorship’. Basically and to go to the grain: Companies have the power, with digital games, to remove them at any time, whether or not you do not, refuse access, and refuse compensation. I mention Concord and The Crew, because they are games that are no longer accessible, and there were people who paid for them days before they were lost. Let us imagine that I buy food for the week, so as not to buy every day, and if the company that made my refrigerator seems to you can stop cooling my food, lose it, and no longer eat it. This is what happens with today’s video games, and that is why I started my abovementioned commentary on a physical game from the Wii, which I can still play, almost 2 decades later. Users do NOT have a voice, vote or any kind of protection against often bad decisions of business leaders. This is, if we are not going to political, undemocratic, but not political, so it is anti-consumer. There is an initiative called ‘Stop Killing Games’, which brought together over 1.5 million signatures from citizens of the European Union, which only calls on businesses to allow users to continue to access the games they have bought. They do not ask for maintenance, no updates, no request for infinite content, no more responsibility than allowing users to access services for which they have paid, after the end of the support. And I mentioned it again, approximately 10 years ago Nintendo stopped supporting Nintendo Wii, and I have not lost my access to it.
